title: Office Avenger
shortDescription: Rapidly neutralize workplace stressors. 10 taps. Maximum catharsis.
description: A proprietary stress-relief velocity assessment disguised as a clinical impulse response test. Tap the workplace aggressor 10 times as fast as neurologically possible.

seo.metaTitle: Office Avenger – Stress Relief Speed Test | NeuroBench
seo.metaDescription: How fast can you neutralize a workplace stressor? Tap the boss 10 times as fast as possible. Measure your stress-relief velocity and share your catharsis score. Free instant test.

howToPlay: Click or tap the 'Begin Protocol' button to initiate the stress-relief assessment.
A workplace stressor stimulus (👹) will appear on screen.
Rapidly tap the stimulus exactly 10 times with maximum neurological urgency.
Your Stress-Relief Velocity is measured in total elapsed milliseconds.
Lower score = faster catharsis = higher workplace survival probability.
content.science: Repetitive motor tapping tasks have been studied extensively in neuroscience as a measure of corticospinal tract efficiency and basal ganglia timing circuits. The speed at which a subject can execute rapid sequential taps reflects the integrity of the supplementary motor area (SMA) and pre-motor cortex, which coordinate timing and sequencing of voluntary movements. Interestingly, research in occupational psychology has found that humor-based stress displacement activities — including symbolic aggression against authority figures — measurably reduce cortisol levels and self-reported workplace stress. This test combines both domains: a genuine measure of motor tapping speed, wrapped in the cathartic framework of office-stress relief.
tips: Use alternating index and middle fingers for maximum tap frequency — a technique called 'trill tapping.'
Keep your wrist elevated and tap from the fingertip only, not the full finger, to reduce travel time.
On mobile, multi-touch with two fingers alternating is significantly faster than single-finger tapping.
Controlled aggression — channeling genuine frustration — has been shown to improve motor output speed by up to 12% in lab conditions.
Visualize your most stressful Monday morning meeting for optimal performance.

title: Memory Matrix
shortDescription: How many digits can your brain hold? Beat the 7-digit average.
description: A clinical measure of your working memory span. Numbers flash briefly — memorize them, then type them back. Each correct answer adds one more digit. How far can you go?

seo.metaTitle: Number Memory Test – Working Memory Capacity Benchmark | NeuroBench
seo.metaDescription: How many digits can you memorize? Test your working memory span online for free. The average person remembers 7 digits — can you beat it? Instant results, no signup.

howToPlay: Click 'Begin Protocol' to start the assessment.
A number sequence will flash on screen — memorize it before it disappears.
Type the number exactly as shown using the on-screen keypad or your keyboard.
Each correct answer adds one more digit to the next sequence.
One mistake ends the test — your score is the longest sequence you recalled correctly.
content.science: Working memory — the brain's mental scratchpad — is one of the most studied constructs in cognitive neuroscience. The digit span task, first formalized by psychologist George Miller in 1956, revealed that humans can hold approximately 7 ± 2 items in short-term memory at once, a finding now known as 'Miller's Law.' The prefrontal cortex and parietal lobes coordinate to maintain and manipulate these temporary representations. Working memory capacity strongly predicts academic performance, fluid intelligence, and professional decision-making speed. Unlike long-term memory, working memory degrades rapidly without active rehearsal — typically within 15–30 seconds. Elite performers in high-stakes professions (surgeons, air traffic controllers, chess grandmasters) consistently score above 9 digits, suggesting working memory is a trainable cognitive resource.
tips: Chunk the digits into groups of 2–3 (e.g. '8 3 7 1 4' → '83' '71' '4') to dramatically extend your span.
Subvocalize the sequence — silently repeating numbers engages the phonological loop, a dedicated working memory subsystem.
Avoid distractions during the flash window — divided attention is the primary cause of encoding failure.
The display time scales with digit count, so longer sequences give you proportionally more time — don't rush.
Practice consistently: working memory span is one of the few cognitive metrics proven to improve with targeted training.

ranks.title: Photographic Memory
ranks.subtitle: Your brain has more RAM than the server

ranks.label: A
ranks.title: High Bandwidth
ranks.subtitle: Remembers everyone's coffee order. Always.

ranks.label: B
ranks.title: Within Normal Range
ranks.subtitle: Miller's Law says you're fine

ranks.title: Slight Buffering
ranks.subtitle: Forgets why they opened the fridge. Daily.

ranks.title: 404: Memory Not Found
ranks.subtitle: Has 47 browser tabs open and counting

title: Sequence Memory
shortDescription: Memorize the pattern. Repeat it back. How long can you go?
description: A clinical assessment of your sequential working memory and pattern recognition. Watch the grid light up — then repeat the exact sequence. Each round adds one more step.

seo.metaTitle: Sequence Memory Test – Pattern Recognition Benchmark | NeuroBench
seo.metaDescription: How long can you follow a growing sequence? Watch the pattern, repeat it back, and benchmark your sequential memory against players worldwide. Free, instant, no signup.

howToPlay: Click 'Begin Protocol' to start the assessment.
Watch the grid — squares will light up in a sequence.
After the sequence finishes, click the squares in the exact same order.
Each correct round adds one more step to the sequence.
One wrong click ends the test — your score is the longest sequence you completed.
content.science: Sequential pattern memory — the ability to encode, retain, and reproduce ordered spatial information — relies on a distributed neural network involving the hippocampus, dorsolateral prefrontal cortex, and the supplementary motor area. This capacity is distinct from simple digit span and is more closely linked to procedural learning and implicit sequence acquisition. Research in cognitive neuroscience demonstrates that sequence memory underlies complex skills including musical performance, surgical technique, and programming. The visuospatial sketchpad, a component of Baddeley's working memory model, is the primary system engaged during this task. Sequence length at which performance breaks down is a reliable predictor of general fluid intelligence and attentional control.
tips: Verbalize the positions as you watch — 'top-left, center, bottom-right' creates a dual-coding memory trace.
Chunk the sequence into groups of 3 once it exceeds 5 steps, treating each chunk as a single unit.
Track the rhythm of the highlights — sequences have timing patterns your motor memory can exploit.
Avoid blinking during the display phase — even a 200ms blink can cause you to miss a flash.
After each round, mentally replay the full sequence before the next display begins to reinforce encoding.

ranks.subtitle: Recites meeting minutes verbatim. Unprompted.

ranks.subtitle: Never forgets a face. Or a grudge.

title: Verbal Memory
shortDescription: SEEN it before or NEW? Keep your streak alive as long as possible.
description: A clinical assessment of your semantic long-term memory. Words appear one at a time — identify whether you've seen them before. One wrong answer ends the protocol.

seo.metaTitle: Verbal Memory Test – Semantic Recognition Benchmark | NeuroBench
seo.metaDescription: Can you remember which words you've seen? Test your verbal memory with our free semantic recognition assessment. One mistake ends it — how long can you last?

howToPlay: Click 'Begin Protocol' to start the assessment.
A word will appear on screen — decide if you've SEEN it before in this session or if it's NEW.
Click the correct button as accurately as possible.
One incorrect answer ends the test immediately.
Your score is the total number of correct identifications before your first mistake.
content.science: Semantic recognition memory — the ability to identify previously encountered verbal stimuli — is a core function of the medial temporal lobe, particularly the hippocampus and perirhinal cortex. This test probes familiarity-based recognition, a signal generated by the perirhinal cortex independently of full recollection. As the word pool grows, the interference between similar items increases, placing greater demands on the prefrontal cortex to resolve conflict between competing memory traces. Verbal memory capacity is one of the earliest cognitive functions to show decline in neurodegenerative conditions, making recognition paradigms like this a clinically validated early-screening tool.
tips: Don't overthink — your first instinct is usually correct. Deliberation introduces false memories.
Focus on unusual or emotionally salient words — they form stronger memory traces.
Avoid looking away between words — sustained attention improves encoding.
If truly unsure, NEW is statistically safer early in the test when the seen pool is small.
Mental fatigue rapidly degrades recognition accuracy — take the test when alert.

ranks.subtitle: Remembers every email thread since 2018

ranks.subtitle: Quotes meeting notes from last quarter

title: Visual Memory
shortDescription: Remember which squares were highlighted. Grids get bigger each round.
description: A clinical measure of your visuospatial working memory capacity. Squares flash briefly — memorize their positions, then click them back. Each round adds more squares and a larger grid.

seo.metaTitle: Visual Memory Test – Visuospatial Working Memory Benchmark | NeuroBench
seo.metaDescription: How many squares can you remember? Test your visual working memory with our free spatial benchmark. Grids get larger each round — how far can you go?

howToPlay: Click 'Begin Protocol' to start.
A grid will appear with some squares highlighted in white — memorize their positions.
The squares will disappear — click all the positions that were highlighted.
Each correct round adds more highlighted squares and increases the grid size.
One wrong click ends the test — your score is the level you reached.
content.science: Visuospatial working memory — the capacity to temporarily store and manipulate spatial location information — is mediated primarily by the right hemisphere parietal and occipital cortices, with executive coordination from the dorsolateral prefrontal cortex. Baddeley's model of working memory identifies a dedicated visuospatial sketchpad subsystem for this function, separate from verbal working memory. Spatial memory capacity is strongly predictive of performance in fields requiring mental rotation and spatial reasoning, including architecture, surgery, and engineering. Impairment in visuospatial working memory is an early marker of several neurodegenerative conditions.
tips: Scan the grid in a consistent pattern (e.g., top-left to bottom-right) to ensure you don't miss any highlighted squares.
Create verbal labels for positions ('top-left corner', 'center') to engage dual-coding memory.
For larger grids, chunk positions into quadrants and memorize each quadrant separately.
Click from memory in the same scanning order you used to study the grid.
Speed is irrelevant during recall — accuracy is everything.

ranks.subtitle: Mentally renders 4K environments in real time

ranks.title: Sharp Vision
ranks.subtitle: Never loses their car in a parking lot

title: Chimp Test
shortDescription: Click the numbers in order before they disappear. Beat the chimps.
description: Inspired by research showing chimpanzees outperform humans in sequential numerical memory. Numbers flash briefly — click them in ascending order before they vanish. How far can you get?

seo.metaTitle: Chimp Test – Sequential Numerical Memory Benchmark | NeuroBench
seo.metaDescription: Chimpanzees beat humans at this test. Can you prove them wrong? Numbers flash briefly — click them in order. Free online chimp memory test, instant results.

howToPlay: Click 'Begin Protocol' to start.
Numbers will appear briefly at random positions on the grid.
After they disappear, click the squares in ascending numerical order (1, 2, 3...).
Each correct round adds one more number.
One wrong click ends the test — your score is the highest level completed.
content.science: This test is inspired by research conducted at the Primate Research Institute of Kyoto University, where chimpanzees demonstrated superior performance to humans in sequential numerical working memory tasks. The study, led by Dr. Tetsuro Matsuzawa, showed that young chimps could memorize and reproduce the positions of numbers 1–9 after a 210ms exposure — faster and more accurately than adult humans. The human disadvantage is thought to stem from our heavy reliance on language-based processing, which introduces interference. This test engages the same visuospatial sketchpad and sequential processing circuits, with performance highly correlated with fluid intelligence scores.
tips: Don't try to read the numbers — scan the entire grid and build a spatial map of all positions simultaneously.
During the flash, fixate on the center of the grid rather than tracking individual numbers.
Click in a smooth spatial arc rather than jumping randomly — motor memory reduces cognitive load.
The key insight: process positions, not values. Your brain is faster at spatial than numerical recall.
Practice the lower levels until they feel automatic — this frees up working memory for the higher numbers.

title: Typing Speed Test
shortDescription: How fast can you type? 60 seconds. Measure your WPM and accuracy.
description: A clinical measure of fine motor automaticity and language processing speed. Type the displayed text as fast and accurately as possible. Your WPM and accuracy are benchmarked globally.

seo.metaTitle: Typing Speed Test – WPM & Accuracy Benchmark | NeuroBench
seo.metaDescription: How fast do you type? Measure your WPM and accuracy in 60 seconds. Free online typing speed test with instant benchmark results. No signup needed.

howToPlay: Click the text area or tap 'Begin Protocol' to start the 60-second assessment.
Type the displayed words as fast and accurately as possible.
Correctly typed words are highlighted green — errors show in red.
The timer starts on your first keystroke.
Your final score shows WPM (words per minute) and accuracy percentage.
content.science: Typing speed reflects the degree of motor sequence automaticity — the extent to which finger movements have been compiled into automatic motor programs that no longer require conscious attention. Expert typists activate motor cortex patterns for entire words rather than individual letters, a phenomenon called chunking. The basal ganglia play a critical role in sequencing these automatic motor routines, while the cerebellum handles timing precision. WPM is highly correlated with overall computer proficiency and is one of the most directly trainable cognitive-motor metrics — professional typists can improve by 20–40 WPM with deliberate practice using the proper touch-typing technique.
tips: Use all ten fingers with proper home-row positioning (ASDF / JKL;) — hunt-and-peck has a hard ceiling around 40 WPM.
Focus on accuracy first — errors cost more time to correct than they save by rushing.
Don't look at your hands — visual feedback from the screen is faster than proprioception.
Type words, not letters — read 1–2 words ahead of what you're currently typing.
Relax your hands between bursts — sustained tension reduces finger independence and slows complex sequences.

title: Color Conflict
shortDescription: The word says RED. The color says GREEN. Which do you click?
description: A clinical measure of inhibitory control based on the Stroop Effect. Color names appear in conflicting ink colors — identify the ink color, not the word. Speed and accuracy both count.

seo.metaTitle: Color Conflict – Stroop Inhibitory Control Test | NeuroBench
seo.metaDescription: Can your brain override what it reads? The Stroop Effect tests your inhibitory control. Click the ink color, not the word. Free online Stroop test with instant results.

howToPlay: A color word will appear in a conflicting ink color (e.g., the word 'RED' printed in green).
Click the button matching the INK COLOR — not the word's meaning.
Respond as fast as possible — both speed and accuracy are measured.
Each correct answer scores a point. One wrong answer ends the test.
Your score is the number of correct answers before your first mistake.
content.science: The Stroop Effect, first documented by John Ridley Stroop in 1935, is one of the most replicated findings in cognitive psychology. When color words are printed in conflicting ink colors, reaction times increase by 100–200ms because reading is a highly automatized process that competes with intentional color identification. The anterior cingulate cortex and prefrontal cortex must actively suppress the prepotent reading response to produce the correct answer. This task is a gold-standard measure of inhibitory control — the executive function responsible for overriding automatic responses — and is used clinically to assess ADHD, frontal lobe integrity, and age-related cognitive decline.
tips: Defocus your eyes slightly to perceive color over meaning — a technique called gestalt processing.
Name the ink color silently before clicking — this engages a parallel verbal pathway that bypasses reading.
Practice improves performance measurably — the prefrontal cortex becomes more efficient at suppressing automatic responses.
Fatigue dramatically worsens Stroop performance — take the test when alert.
Don't rush — accuracy matters more than raw speed in this assessment.

ranks.title: Inhibition Master
ranks.subtitle: Never accidentally sends the wrong Slack reaction

ranks.label: A
ranks.title: Focused Executive
ranks.subtitle: Reads the fine print. Actually.

ranks.label: B
ranks.title: Controlled Thinker
ranks.subtitle: Occasionally gets tricked. Like a normal person.

ranks.label: C

ranks.subtitle: Has sent sounds good to an email they didn't read

title: Instant Comparison
shortDescription: Two equations. One is bigger. Click the larger side as fast as possible.
description: A clinical measure of numerical magnitude processing speed. Two math expressions appear side by side — identify the larger value as fast as your brain allows.

seo.metaTitle: Instant Comparison – Numerical Processing Speed Test | NeuroBench
seo.metaDescription: How fast can your brain compare numbers? Two equations appear — click the larger side as fast as possible. Free numerical magnitude processing benchmark.

howToPlay: Two math expressions will appear side-by-side (e.g., 7 + 3 vs 12 - 4).
Click the side with the HIGHER numerical value as fast as possible.
Both speed and accuracy are measured — wrong answers end the test.
Expressions get progressively harder as your streak grows.
Your score is the number of correct comparisons before your first mistake.
content.science: Numerical magnitude comparison activates the intraparietal sulcus (IPS), a region specialized for representing numerical quantity on a mental number line. This approximate number system (ANS) is present in infants and non-human primates, suggesting it is a core evolutionary cognitive capacity. Response time follows Weber's Law: close numerical values take longer to discriminate than distant ones. Individual differences in ANS acuity are significant predictors of formal mathematical achievement across the lifespan.
tips: Estimate, don't calculate — your ANS is faster than deliberate arithmetic for obvious contrasts.
For close values, prioritize scanning the dominant operands quickly.
Subtraction slows most people — practice spotting negative contributions at a glance.
If both sides look similar, trust your gut — ANS intuition is often faster than conscious calculation.
Fixate center to catch both expressions in peripheral vision simultaneously.

ranks.title: Quantitative Oracle
ranks.subtitle: Does ROI calculations in their head mid-meeting

ranks.label: A
ranks.title: Sharp Analyst
ranks.subtitle: Catches spreadsheet errors before they're sent

ranks.label: B
ranks.title: Reliable Estimator
ranks.subtitle: Splits bills without a calculator. Mostly.

ranks.label: C
ranks.title: Approximate Thinker
ranks.subtitle: Always says let me circle back on the numbers

ranks.label: D
ranks.title: Innumerate
ranks.subtitle: Leaves a 10% tip on a $200 bill. Accidentally.

title: Angle Precision
shortDescription: Rotate the line to match the reference. Accuracy in degrees is your score.
description: A clinical measure of visuospatial precision and fine motor control. Drag to rotate a line to be exactly parallel to a reference line. Your error in degrees determines your rank.

seo.metaTitle: Angle Precision – Visuospatial Accuracy Benchmark | NeuroBench
seo.metaDescription: How precise is your spatial perception? Rotate a line to match the reference angle. Your error in degrees determines your rank. Free visuospatial benchmark.

howToPlay: A reference line will appear at a fixed angle on screen.
A second line starts at a random angle — drag or use the slider to rotate it.
Try to make both lines exactly parallel.
Click Submit when you are satisfied with your alignment.
Complete 5 rounds — your average error in degrees across all rounds is your final score.
content.science: Visuospatial orientation judgment relies on the right parietal cortex, particularly the superior parietal lobule, which maintains an internal model of spatial relationships. The precision of angle discrimination follows Weber's Law at small angles. Architects, surgeons, pilots, and artists consistently outperform average populations on spatial orientation tasks. Fine motor control for precise rotational adjustment recruits the cerebellum's error-correction circuits, making this task a combined measure of perceptual acuity and motor precision.
tips: Step back from the screen — peripheral vision integrates angles more holistically than foveal vision.
Use small increments near your target — overshoot and correct rather than stopping short.
Anchor your estimate to the nearest horizontal or vertical — cardinal directions are easiest.
Close one eye to eliminate binocular depth cues that can distort angular perception.
Relax your wrist before adjusting — muscle tension creates micro-tremors.

ranks.title: Spatial Surgeon
ranks.subtitle: Hangs pictures perfectly on the first try. Always.

ranks.label: A
ranks.title: Precision Engineer
ranks.subtitle: Their PowerPoint arrows actually align

ranks.label: B
ranks.title: Decent Navigator
ranks.subtitle: Owns a level. Uses a level.

title: Rapid Scan
shortDescription: Find the Q hiding among hundreds of O's. As fast as possible.
description: A clinical measure of visual search efficiency and pre-attentive processing. A single target character hides among hundreds of distractors — find it as fast as possible. Density increases each round.

seo.metaTitle: Rapid Scan – Visual Search Speed Test | NeuroBench
seo.metaDescription: Find the Q among hundreds of O's as fast as possible. Test your visual search speed and pre-attentive processing. Free online visual attention benchmark.

howToPlay: The screen fills with a grid of identical O characters.
One Q is hidden somewhere in the grid — find it and click it as fast as possible.
Each successful round increases the grid density.
Your score is the number of rounds completed.
Clicking the wrong character ends the test immediately.
content.science: Visual search tasks distinguish between pre-attentive and attentive processing. When targets share features with distractors (as in Q-among-O), search requires serial attentive scanning — reaction time scales linearly with distractor count. The Q-in-O task engages feature conjunction search, requiring the visual system to combine information about curvature and the presence of a tail. This recruits the frontal eye fields and parietal cortex in a coordinated top-down attention sweep.
tips: Don't scan systematically — let your eyes roam freely. Pre-attentive processing works best with distributed attention.
Slightly blur your focus — the Q tail becomes more salient in peripheral vision.
Scan in a loose Z-pattern to maximize grid coverage efficiently.
Increase screen brightness — higher contrast makes the subtle Q-tail more detectable.
Avoid fixating on individual characters — move your gaze in large jumps.

ranks.title: Eagle Eye
ranks.subtitle: Spots typos in slide decks from across the room

ranks.label: A
ranks.title: Sharp Scanner
ranks.subtitle: Finds Waldo without looking like they're trying

ranks.label: B

ranks.subtitle: Usually spots the different one

title: Temporal Pulse
shortDescription: The beat stops. You continue it. How precise is your internal clock?
description: A clinical measure of internal timing precision. Three pulses flash at 1000ms intervals — then you must tap the hidden 4th beat. Your delta from perfect timing determines your rank.

seo.metaTitle: Temporal Pulse – Internal Clock Precision Test | NeuroBench
seo.metaDescription: How precise is your internal sense of time? Watch 3 flashes at 1000ms intervals, then tap the hidden 4th beat. Free timing precision benchmark.

howToPlay: Watch three flashes appear at exactly 1000ms intervals — internalize the rhythm.
After the third flash, the screen goes dark — tap when you think the 4th beat occurs.
Your error is the difference in milliseconds between your tap and the perfect 1000ms mark.
Complete 5 rounds — your average error is your final score.
Lower error = higher rank. S tier requires less than 15ms average deviation.
content.science: Internal timing precision relies on the basal ganglia and supplementary motor area (SMA), which form a dedicated neural clock for interval timing. Beat induction additionally engages the cerebellum's error-prediction circuits. Individual differences in temporal precision correlate strongly with musical ability, athletic timing, and reading fluency. Expert musicians and drummers achieve sub-10ms timing precision — approaching the limits of the nervous system's temporal resolution.
tips: Count internally with the flashes then continue on the beat — verbal encoding helps.
Tap slightly early rather than late — humans naturally anticipate beats slightly late.
Tap with your index finger, hold your hand still — body movement introduces variability.
Try tapping along with all three flashes before the hidden beat — motor entrainment improves accuracy.
Breathe steadily — breath-holding distorts time perception.

ranks.label: S
ranks.title: Human Metronome
ranks.subtitle: Arrives at meetings exactly on time. To the second.

ranks.label: A
ranks.title: Precision Timer
ranks.subtitle: Their Pomodoros actually last 25 minutes

ranks.label: B
ranks.title: Rhythmic Thinker
ranks.subtitle: Gives accurate ETAs. Occasionally.

ranks.label: C
ranks.title: Approximate Clocker
ranks.subtitle: Says 5 minutes and means 20

ranks.label: D

ranks.subtitle: Has no idea what time it is. Ever.

title: Don't Blink
shortDescription: Something on screen will change. Catch it the instant it happens.
description: A clinical measure of visual vigilance and change detection speed. An object is displayed — at a random moment it subtly changes. Click the instant you notice. Changes get more subtle each round.

seo.metaTitle: Don't Blink – Change Detection Visual Vigilance Test | NeuroBench
seo.metaDescription: Something will change on screen. Can you catch it? Test your visual vigilance and change detection speed. Free online attention benchmark with instant results.

howToPlay: An object will appear on screen — stare at it without blinking.
At a random moment between 1–4 seconds, something will subtly change.
Click or tap the instant you notice the change.
If you miss it, the round counts as a miss — you get 10 rounds total.
Your score combines average reaction speed and detection accuracy.
content.science: Change blindness — the failure to detect visual changes that occur during brief interruptions — is one of the most striking demonstrations of the limits of human visual attention. Even large changes can go unnoticed when attention is divided or misdirected. The phenomenon reveals that visual perception is not a passive recording of the scene but an active, attention-dependent process. The superior colliculus and V5/MT area of the visual cortex are responsible for detecting motion and change signals, but these signals only reach conscious awareness when top-down attention from the prefrontal cortex is directed to the relevant location. This test specifically measures the efficiency of that attentional spotlight.
tips: Fixate on the center of the object — do not let your gaze drift.
Blink as little as possible — the change may occur during a blink.
Broaden your attentional focus rather than fixating on one feature.
Changes are always in one of three domains: color, number, or position.
At higher rounds, changes become extremely subtle — trust your gut rather than deliberating.

ranks.label: S
ranks.title: Eagle Cortex
ranks.subtitle: Notices your haircut. Every. Single. Time.

ranks.label: A
ranks.title: Sharp Vigilance
ranks.subtitle: Spots the typo before the email is sent

ranks.label: B
ranks.title: Attentive Observer
ranks.subtitle: Usually catches the important stuff

ranks.label: C
ranks.title: Selective Notifier
ranks.subtitle: Walked past a fire alarm for three days

ranks.label: D
ranks.title: Change Blind
ranks.subtitle: Didn't notice their own haircut in the mirror

title: Count Master
shortDescription: Dots flash for half a second. How many were there?
description: A clinical measure of subitizing ability and approximate number system acuity. Dots flash briefly — estimate the count as accurately as possible. Accuracy and average error determine your rank.

seo.metaTitle: Count Master – Subitizing & Number Estimation Test | NeuroBench
seo.metaDescription: Dots flash for 0.5 seconds — how many were there? Test your subitizing ability and approximate number sense. Free online benchmark with instant results.

howToPlay: A cluster of dots will flash on screen for exactly 0.5 seconds.
Enter how many dots you saw using the keypad.
Submit your answer before the next round begins.
Complete 10 rounds — your accuracy percentage and average error are your scores.
Dots range from 5 to 20 — estimates, not exact counts, are expected for larger numbers.
content.science: Subitizing — from the Latin 'subitus' (sudden) — is the rapid, accurate, pre-attentive perception of small quantities (typically 1–4 items). For quantities above 4, the brain switches to estimation via the Approximate Number System (ANS), a phylogenetically ancient capacity shared with many non-human species. ANS acuity follows Weber's Law: the just-noticeable difference between two quantities is a constant ratio rather than a fixed number. Individual ANS acuity, measured by tasks like this, is a robust predictor of formal mathematical achievement from kindergarten through university.
tips: For 5 or fewer dots, do not count — let subitizing work instantly.
For larger quantities, look for spatial patterns: rows, columns, clusters.
Guess slightly higher than your estimate — humans systematically undercount in brief exposures.
Do not try to count individually — the flash is too brief. Trust your gestalt impression.
Practice improves ANS acuity measurably over weeks of regular training.

title: Neural Latency Test
shortDescription: Measure your synaptic transmission speed in milliseconds.
description: A clinical-grade assessment of your visual cortex processing speed and motor neuron response time. Used by elite athletes and neuroscience researchers worldwide.

seo.metaTitle: Neural Latency Test – Free Online Reaction Time Benchmark
seo.metaDescription: Measure your reaction time with clinical precision. Click when green appears and benchmark your neural response speed against thousands of high-performers worldwide. Free, instant, no signup.

howToPlay: Click or tap anywhere on the stimulus panel to initiate the protocol.
The panel will display a red standby indicator — remain focused and do not interact.
The moment the panel switches to green, click or tap as fast as possible.
Your Neural Latency score is displayed in milliseconds (ms).
Share your Neuro-Bench Report Card and challenge your colleagues.
content.science: Neural latency — commonly called reaction time — measures the total elapsed time from sensory stimulus to motor response initiation. The signal travels from your retina through the optic nerve to the primary visual cortex (V1), is processed by the dorsal visual stream, then a motor command is dispatched through the corticospinal tract to your finger flexor muscles. The entire cascade takes approximately 150–300ms in healthy adults. Elite athletes, fighter pilots, and professional gamers consistently score below 200ms through deliberate neuromuscular training. Fatigue, dehydration, alcohol, and age all measurably degrade performance. A single well-rested morning session after caffeine intake typically produces a subject's personal best scores, as adrenergic stimulation accelerates cortical processing speed.
tips: Use your dominant index finger — it has the most direct motor pathway to the primary motor cortex.
Do not attempt to anticipate the green signal — pre-motor activation invalidates the measurement.
Perform 1–2 warm-up taps before beginning to prime your neuromuscular junction.
A wired input device eliminates Bluetooth polling latency (~8ms) for the most accurate score.
Controlled diaphragmatic breathing before the test measurably lowers sympathetic arousal and improves baseline latency.

title: Report or Favor
shortDescription: Work task or personal favor? You have 1.5 seconds to decide.
description: Requests are flying in from your boss. Real work tasks must be ACCEPTED. Outrageous personal favors must be DODGED. Respond fast — hesitation is not a corporate survival skill.

seo.metaTitle: Report or Favor – Office Survival Reaction Test | NeuroBench
seo.metaDescription: Your boss keeps sending requests. Work task or personal favor? You have 1.5 seconds to decide. Free office survival reaction test with instant results.

howToPlay: Requests fall from the top of the screen one at a time.
Tap ACCEPT for real work tasks — reports, meetings, deadlines.
Tap DODGE for personal favors — anything your boss has absolutely no right to ask.
You have 1.5 seconds per request. Hesitate and you're done.
One wrong answer ends the test. Your streak is your score.
content.science: Rapid categorization under time pressure activates the anterior cingulate cortex and lateral prefrontal cortex — the same neural circuits engaged in conflict monitoring and response inhibition. Workplace boundary recognition requires simultaneous evaluation of social norms, professional context, and personal risk — a complex executive function task dressed up as reflexive response. Individuals with stronger inhibitory control and faster categorical processing make boundary decisions more quickly and accurately, with less post-decisional regret.
tips: Anything involving the boss's home, car, pets, family, or weekend is always a DODGE.
Anything with a deadline, a report name, or a client is always an ACCEPT.
Do not overthink — your first instinct is calibrated by years of workplace exposure.
The requests escalate in absurdity as your streak grows — trust the obvious.
Speed matters as much as accuracy — a correct answer after 1.4 seconds is barely a win.

shortDescription: Catch the reports. Dodge the boss. 30 seconds. 3 lives.
description: Reports and boss emojis rain down the screen. Tap every report you see — but do NOT tap the boss. Three boss taps and you're fired. Speed increases with your score.

seo.metaTitle: Boss Dodge – Office Survival Tap Game | NeuroBench
seo.metaDescription: Reports rain down — tap them all. But don't tap the boss! 30 seconds, 3 lives. Free office survival reflex game with instant results.

howToPlay: Objects fall from the top of the screen in multiple lanes.
Tap every 📋 report you see to catch it — each one scores a point.
Do NOT tap the 👹 boss — three boss taps and the test terminates.
Objects fall faster as your score increases.
Survive 30 seconds and catch as many reports as possible.
content.science: Selective attention under time pressure — the ability to respond to target stimuli while suppressing responses to distractors — is a core executive function mediated by the prefrontal cortex and anterior cingulate cortex. This task is structurally identical to clinical go/no-go paradigms used to assess impulse control and attentional selectivity. The increasing speed manipulation places additional demands on processing efficiency, mimicking the neural load experienced under genuine time pressure in professional environments.
tips: Focus on the lower third of the screen — by the time objects reach there, you have enough information to respond.
Use your peripheral vision to track multiple lanes simultaneously.
The boss emoji is visually distinct — train yourself to react to its absence rather than its presence.
At high speeds, accept that you will miss some reports — the priority is boss avoidance.
Tap with your index finger and keep your hand centered for fastest cross-lane reach.

title: Raise or Raise
shortDescription: Your salary is rising. Lock it in at the peak. Don't get greedy.
description: Your annual salary climbs on screen. It will peak at a random moment then drop. Hit LOCK IT IN at the highest point you can — but wait too long and it crashes. 5 rounds. Average salary is your score.

seo.metaTitle: Raise or Raise – Salary Negotiation Timing Game | NeuroBench
seo.metaDescription: Your salary is climbing. Lock it in at the peak before it crashes. Test your negotiation timing instinct. Free office survival game with instant results.

howToPlay: Your salary starts at $80,000 and begins climbing.
The number rises at a random rate and peaks at an unpredictable moment.
Hit LOCK IT IN when you think you've reached the peak.
Wait too long and the salary drops — whatever you locked in is your round score.
Complete 5 rounds — your average locked salary is your final score.
content.science: Peak detection under uncertainty is a classic problem in optimal stopping theory — the mathematical framework underlying real-world decisions from hiring to negotiation. The optimal strategy for maximizing expected value in such tasks is related to the secretary problem: observe a fixed fraction of options, then commit to the next option that exceeds all previous observations. In salary negotiation contexts, research shows that anchoring and loss aversion systematically bias humans away from optimal stopping, causing both premature commitment and excessive holding. This test measures your intuitive calibration against theoretical optima.
tips: The peak is random — there is no pattern to learn. Pure instinct.
Statistically, locking in when the number feels 'about right' outperforms both early and late strategies.
Loss aversion will make you hold too long — resist the urge to squeeze out the last few thousand.
Each round is independent — a bad round does not predict the next.
The optimal stopping rule: lock in after the salary has risen for at least 2 seconds.

title: Corporate Climber
shortDescription: Collect promotions. Dodge the chaos. How far up the ladder can you climb?
description: Navigate the corporate maze. Collect promotions and bonuses to grow your career. Avoid personal favors, overtime demands, and angry bosses — they block your path permanently. How high can you climb?

seo.metaTitle: Corporate Climber – Office Snake Survival Game | NeuroBench
seo.metaDescription: Collect promotions. Dodge overtime and angry bosses. A corporate twist on the classic snake game. Free office survival game with instant results.

howToPlay: Use arrow keys (desktop) or swipe (mobile) to navigate your career path.
Collect 🏆 promotions and ⭐ bonuses to grow your career length.
Avoid 📋 personal favors, 💼 overtime demands, and 😤 angry bosses.
Hitting any obstacle turns it into a permanent wall — plan your route.
Your score is your maximum career length. How far up can you climb?
content.science: Navigation tasks requiring simultaneous path planning and obstacle avoidance engage the hippocampal-entorhinal circuit for spatial mapping, the prefrontal cortex for prospective planning, and the basal ganglia for action sequence selection. The snake paradigm specifically tests an aspect of working memory called prospective interference — the ability to inhibit future collision paths while maintaining current trajectory. Performance on spatial navigation tasks of this type correlates with fluid intelligence and executive function battery scores.
tips: Always plan 3–4 moves ahead — reacting only to the immediate cell leads to dead ends.
Hug the walls early to maximize open space in the center.
Never turn back on yourself — reversals guarantee immediate collision.
Prioritize path preservation over item collection at higher lengths.
On mobile, swipe decisively — a partial swipe registers as no input.

title: Color Blind Test
shortDescription: Do you see the number hidden in the dots? The world's most recognized color vision test.
description: A digital adaptation of the Ishihara color vision test. Colored dot patterns conceal numbers — people with normal color vision see them instantly. Color blind individuals cannot. Find out in 60 seconds.

seo.metaTitle: Free Color Blind Test – Ishihara Color Vision Test Online | ZAZAZA
seo.metaDescription: Take the free Ishihara color blind test online. Find out if you have color vision deficiency in 60 seconds. No signup. Instant results.

howToPlay: A circular pattern of colored dots will appear on screen.
A number is hidden within the dots — visible to people with normal color vision.
Enter the number you see using the keypad. If you see nothing, enter 0.
Complete all rounds — your color vision profile is calculated at the end.
Results show which color channels may be affected.
content.science: The Ishihara color vision test, developed by Dr. Shinobu Ishihara in 1917, uses pseudoisochromatic plates — dot patterns where numbers are distinguishable by hue but not by luminance. Red-green color blindness (deuteranopia and protanopia) affects approximately 8% of males and 0.5% of females of Northern European descent. The condition results from absence or malfunction of cone photoreceptors in the retina. This digital adaptation uses the same psychophysical principles as the clinical plates.
tips: View the test in good lighting — avoid dimly lit rooms.
Don't zoom in — view at normal screen distance.
If you're uncertain, go with your first impression.
Some plates test for different color blindness types — answer what you genuinely see.
Color blindness is hereditary and cannot be corrected by practice.

title: Contrast Sensitivity
shortDescription: Can you see the faint pattern? Your contrast sensitivity reveals how well your vision handles low-light and aging.
description: A measure of your visual system's ability to detect differences in luminance. Patterns fade progressively — how faint can you still detect? Contrast sensitivity declines before standard acuity, making it an early indicator of visual aging.

seo.metaTitle: Free Contrast Sensitivity Test – Visual Acuity Eye Age | ZAZAZA
seo.metaDescription: Test your contrast sensitivity online. Fading patterns reveal your visual system's detection threshold. Free eye age test, instant results, no signup.

howToPlay: A striped grating pattern will appear at decreasing contrast levels.
Indicate whether you can see a pattern (YES) or whether the screen looks blank (NO).
The contrast decreases with each correct detection — find your threshold.
Complete all rounds — your contrast sensitivity score is calculated.
Lower threshold = better vision.
content.science: Contrast sensitivity function (CSF) measures the visual system's ability to detect spatial luminance differences across a range of spatial frequencies. Unlike standard Snellen acuity (20/20), CSF captures the full range of visual performance. Contrast sensitivity begins declining in the mid-30s and accelerates after 60, often before any decline in standard acuity is detectable. It is used clinically to assess early cataracts, glaucoma, and amblyopia.
tips: Ensure your screen brightness is set to maximum for accurate results.
Test in a moderately lit room — not too bright, not too dark.
Blink naturally — eye strain degrades sensitivity.
If you wear glasses or contacts, wear them during the test.
Results may vary by screen quality — IPS panels provide the most accurate results.

title: Hue Ordering Test
shortDescription: Arrange the colors in perfect order. How precisely can you distinguish hue? Your color IQ revealed.
description: A simplified adaptation of the Farnsworth-Munsell 100 Hue Test. Arrange color chips in continuous hue order — the number of errors reveals your color discrimination precision. Top scorers have near-tetrachromatic color sensitivity.

seo.metaTitle: Free Hue Ordering Test – Color Discrimination & Eye Age | ZAZAZA
seo.metaDescription: Take the free Farnsworth-Munsell inspired hue ordering test. How well can you arrange colors? Instant results, no signup. Test your color IQ.

howToPlay: Focus on the center of the screen.
When a visual stimulus appears, click or tap as fast as possible.
Complete 10 rounds — your average visual reaction time is your score.
Your reaction time is compared to population norms by age group.
Lower milliseconds = younger visual processing age.
content.science: Visual reaction time integrates retinal processing speed, optic nerve conduction velocity, and primary visual cortex response time. Unlike simple reaction time tests, this protocol uses specific visual stimulus parameters to isolate the visual processing component. Research shows visual RT increases approximately 1ms per year from age 25, accelerating after 60. The test provides an estimate of your visual processing age relative to population norms.
tips: Fixate on the center of the screen — peripheral detection is slower.
Blink before each trial to refresh your visual field.
Avoid caffeine deficit — visual processing speed is sensitive to arousal state.
Screen refresh rate matters — 60Hz+ displays give most accurate results.
Test in consistent lighting conditions for repeatable results.

title: Attention Span Test
shortDescription: How long can you stay focused without missing a target? Test your sustained attention span.
description: A clinical measure of sustained attention and vigilance. Targets appear among distractors over a continuous period — miss too many and your attention span is flagged. How long can you maintain peak focus?

seo.metaTitle: Free Attention Span Test – Focus & ADHD Screening | ZAZAZA
seo.metaDescription: Test your attention span and sustained focus online. Free attention test with ADHD insights. How long can you stay focused? Instant results, no signup.

howToPlay: Targets will appear on screen at random intervals among distractors.
Tap or click only the target — not the distractors.
Maintain focus for the full 90-second session.
Your score is accuracy % combined with reaction consistency.
Attention lapses (missed targets or false alarms) are tracked separately.
content.science: Sustained attention — the ability to maintain focused alertness over time — is mediated by the right prefrontal cortex and anterior cingulate cortex, with arousal modulation from the locus coeruleus-norepinephrine system. The Continuous Performance Test (CPT), on which this assessment is based, is the gold-standard clinical measure for ADHD screening. Attention performance peaks at ages 20-35 and shows gradual decline thereafter. Individual differences in sustained attention strongly predict academic and professional performance.
tips: Minimize external distractions before starting — close other tabs.
Sit upright — posture affects alertness and prefrontal cortex activity.
Avoid testing when fatigued — sustained attention is highly sensitive to sleep debt.
Focus on consistency, not speed — accuracy matters more than reaction time here.
If you notice your mind wandering, gently refocus without judgment.

title: Distraction Shield
shortDescription: Find the target while ignoring everything else. How resistant are you to distraction?
description: A measure of selective attention and interference resistance. Visual noise surrounds the target — your job is to filter it out and respond only to what matters. The interference grows with each level.

seo.metaTitle: Free Distraction Shield Test – Selective Attention | ZAZAZA
seo.metaDescription: Test your resistance to distraction. Find the target in visual noise. Free selective attention test, instant results, no signup.

howToPlay: A target symbol will appear among distractors on screen.
Tap the target as fast as possible while ignoring all distractors.
Distractors increase in number and similarity each round.
Your score is levels completed before accuracy drops below threshold.
Speed and accuracy are both measured.
content.science: Selective attention — the ability to focus on relevant stimuli while filtering irrelevant ones — is mediated by top-down control from the prefrontal cortex and bottom-up salience signals from the parietal cortex. The ability to suppress interference is a core component of executive function and strongly predicts academic performance, driving safety, and professional decision-making quality. Distractor interference increases with similarity to the target — a phenomenon called the flanker effect, first described by Eriksen & Eriksen (1974).
tips: Fixate on the center of the display — let peripheral vision flag the target.
Don't scan systematically — allow your visual system to detect the target pre-attentively.
Resist the urge to confirm — trust your first identification.
Maintain steady breathing — anxiety narrows attentional spotlight and increases false alarms.
The more similar distractors look to the target, the more processing power you need.

title: Task Switch Speed
shortDescription: Switch between tasks as fast as possible. Your switching cost reveals your mental flexibility.
description: Measures cognitive flexibility — how quickly your brain can reconfigure itself for a new task. Alternating rules require you to switch mental sets rapidly. The switching cost (delay vs. non-switch) is your key metric.

seo.metaTitle: Free Task Switch Speed Test – Cognitive Flexibility | ZAZAZA
seo.metaDescription: Test your cognitive flexibility with the task switching test. How quickly can your brain switch between rules? Free executive function test, instant results.

howToPlay: Two alternating rules appear — follow the rule shown on screen.
Switch between rules as fast as possible when prompted.
Your reaction time on switch trials vs. non-switch trials is compared.
Your switching cost (the delay caused by switching) is your score.
Lower switching cost = greater cognitive flexibility.
content.science: Task-switching cost — the additional reaction time incurred when switching between tasks compared to repeating the same task — is a reliable measure of cognitive flexibility and executive control. Mediated by the prefrontal cortex and anterior cingulate cortex, task switching requires active inhibition of the previous task set and retrieval of the new one. Switching costs decrease with practice and are larger in older adults, individuals with ADHD, and under cognitive load. The ability to switch rapidly between tasks predicts multitasking performance and professional adaptability.
tips: Read the rule cue before the stimulus appears — preparation reduces switching cost.
Don't carry over the previous rule — fully reset between trials.
Consistent rhythm helps — irregular pacing increases switching cost.
Cognitive flexibility improves with dual-task practice and novel learning.
Switching cost naturally increases with fatigue — test when alert.

title: Dark Triad Score
shortDescription: Narcissism, Machiavellianism, Psychopathy. How dark is your personality? Be honest.
description: The Dark Triad measures three subclinical personality traits: narcissism (entitlement and grandiosity), Machiavellianism (strategic manipulation), and psychopathy (callousness and impulsivity). Most people score somewhere on each dimension. Find out where you land.

seo.metaTitle: Free Dark Triad Test – Narcissism, Psychopathy & Machiavellianism | ZAZAZA
seo.metaDescription: Take the free Dark Triad personality test. Measure your narcissism, Machiavellianism and psychopathy scores. Instant results. No signup. Are you ready for the truth?

howToPlay: Answer 27 questions honestly — there are no right or wrong answers.
Rate each statement from 1 (strongly disagree) to 5 (strongly agree).
Do not overthink — your first instinct is most accurate.
Your scores on Narcissism, Machiavellianism, and Psychopathy are calculated separately.
Results show your profile relative to population norms.
content.science: The Dark Triad, conceptualized by Paulhus and Williams (2002), identifies three overlapping but distinct personality constructs that share a tendency toward social aversion. Narcissism is measured using the Narcissistic Personality Inventory (NPI); Machiavellianism via the Mach-IV scale; and psychopathy via the Self-Report Psychopathy scale. Subclinical levels of these traits are found throughout the general population and correlate with leadership emergence, risk tolerance, and persuasion effectiveness — as well as interpersonal conflict and ethical violations.
tips: Answer based on your genuine tendencies, not your ideal self.
High scores on any dimension are not inherently negative — context matters.
Narcissism at moderate levels correlates with confidence and leadership.
Machiavellianism predicts strategic thinking and negotiation skill.
This test measures subclinical traits, not clinical personality disorders.

title: Empathy Score
shortDescription: How well do you read other people's emotions? Your empathy score reveals your social intelligence.
description: Measures both cognitive empathy (understanding others' mental states) and affective empathy (sharing others' emotional experiences). High empathy predicts social connection quality; low empathy predicts Dark Triad tendencies. Where do you fall?

seo.metaTitle: Free Empathy Score Test – Emotional & Social Intelligence | ZAZAZA
seo.metaDescription: Test your empathy score online. Measure cognitive and affective empathy. Free emotional intelligence test. How well do you read other people? Instant results.

howToPlay: You will be shown faces, scenarios, and statements.
Identify the emotion displayed or choose how you would feel in each scenario.
Answer quickly and honestly — your first response is usually most accurate.
Your cognitive empathy and affective empathy scores are calculated separately.
Results compare your profile to population norms.
content.science: Empathy comprises two distinct components: cognitive empathy (theory of mind — the ability to infer others' mental states) and affective empathy (emotional contagion — the tendency to share others' feelings). Cognitive empathy is mediated by the temporoparietal junction and medial prefrontal cortex; affective empathy by the anterior insula and anterior cingulate cortex. Baron-Cohen's Empathy Quotient (EQ) is the most widely used research tool in this domain. Empathy scores correlate inversely with Dark Triad traits and predict prosocial behavior, relationship quality, and therapeutic effectiveness.
tips: Don't try to give the 'right' answer — empathy tests are most accurate when answered instinctively.
High affective empathy is not always adaptive — it can lead to empathy fatigue.
Cognitive empathy without affective empathy is associated with Machiavellianism.
Affective empathy without cognitive empathy can lead to emotional overwhelm.
Empathy is partially heritable but also highly trainable through deliberate social practice.

ranks.title: Empath
ranks.subtitle: Feels everyone's feelings. Exhausting but beautiful.

ranks.title: Highly Attuned

ranks.subtitle: Understands feelings intellectually but doesn't feel them

ranks.label: D
ranks.title: Empathy Deficit
ranks.subtitle: Asks 'are you okay?' while already walking away

title: Manipulation Detector
shortDescription: Can you spot manipulation when you see it? Or are you the one doing it?
description: Tests both your ability to detect manipulative behavior in others AND your own tendency toward Machiavellian reasoning. Two sides of the same coin — your score reveals which side of the chess board you're on.

seo.metaTitle: Free Manipulation Detector Test – Dark Psychology | ZAZAZA
seo.metaDescription: Can you spot manipulation? Or are you the manipulator? Free dark psychology test measuring Machiavellian reasoning and deception detection. Instant results.

howToPlay: Read scenarios describing social interactions.
Identify the manipulative element — or rate how you would respond.
Answer honestly — the test detects both detection skill and personal tendency.
Your Detector Score (how well you spot manipulation) and your Operator Score (tendency to use it) are calculated separately.
Most people are higher on one than the other.
content.science: Machiavellianism — named for Niccolò Machiavelli's treatise on political strategy — describes a personality disposition characterized by strategic interpersonal manipulation, cynical worldview, and long-term self-interest orientation. The Mach-IV scale (Christie & Geis, 1970) is the foundational measure. High Machiavellians are skilled at identifying and exploiting social leverage points, making them effective negotiators and political operators. Deception detection ability is correlated but distinct — research shows most people perform only slightly above chance at detecting lies, regardless of Mach score.
tips: Look for incongruence between stated motives and actual outcomes in scenarios.
Manipulation often involves flattery, urgency, and appeals to reciprocity.
High Mach scorers often project their own tendencies onto others.
Deception detection improves with awareness of common manipulation patterns.
Answer the personal tendency questions about your actual behavior, not your ideal.

ranks.label: S
ranks.title: Grand Operator
ranks.subtitle: Sees seventeen moves ahead and three chess boards at once

ranks.label: A
ranks.title: Strategic Thinker
ranks.subtitle: Spots the angle in every conversation

ranks.label: B
ranks.title: Situationally Aware
ranks.subtitle: Suspects something's off but can't prove it

ranks.label: C
ranks.title: Trusting
ranks.subtitle: Takes things at face value. Endearing.

ranks.label: D
ranks.title: Fully Transparent
ranks.subtitle: Has never had an ulterior motive in their life

title: Vocabulary Age
shortDescription: How rich is your vocabulary? Your word knowledge reveals your verbal brain age.
description: Vocabulary size is one of the strongest predictors of verbal intelligence, educational attainment, and reading comprehension. This test measures the breadth of your lexical knowledge to estimate your vocabulary age relative to population norms.

seo.metaTitle: Free Vocabulary Age Test – Word Knowledge & Verbal IQ | ZAZAZA
seo.metaDescription: Find your vocabulary age. Test your word knowledge and verbal intelligence. Free vocab age test with instant results. How old is your vocabulary? No signup.

howToPlay: Words will appear on screen — some real, some invented.
Indicate whether each word is a real English word (YES) or not (NO).
You will also be asked to identify definitions for some words.
Answer as fast as you can — hesitation may indicate word unfamiliarity.
Your vocabulary size estimate and vocabulary age are calculated from your performance.
content.science: Vocabulary size in adults ranges from approximately 20,000 to 100,000+ word families, with the average educated adult knowing around 40,000-60,000 words. The Vocabulary Levels Test (Nation, 1990) and the Vocabulary Size Test (Nation & Beglar, 2007) are foundational research instruments. Vocabulary is highly correlated with crystallized intelligence (Gc) and continues growing throughout adulthood, unlike most other cognitive abilities. It is the single strongest predictor of academic success and is highly valued in high-income professional contexts, driving premium advertising CPM rates.
tips: Don't guess randomly on invented words — false positive errors reduce your score.
If a word looks familiar, it probably is — trust lexical familiarity signals.
Vocabulary grows with reading — breadth of reading material matters more than volume.
Technical vocabulary in your professional domain inflates scores in that register.
Vocabulary age continues to grow throughout life — unlike most cognitive metrics.

ranks.label: S
ranks.title: Lexical Genius
ranks.subtitle: Uses words others have to Google

ranks.label: A
ranks.title: Word Wizard
ranks.subtitle: Always has the exact right word. Always.

ranks.label: B
ranks.title: Articulate
ranks.subtitle: Expresses ideas clearly. Usually.

ranks.label: C
ranks.title: Average Vocabulary
ranks.subtitle: Gets the point across. Eventually.

ranks.label: D
ranks.title: Word Challenged
ranks.subtitle: Uses 'thing' as a noun for everything

title: Word Recognition Speed
clinicalTitle: Orthographic Processing & Lexical Access Speed Assessment
shortDescription: How fast can your brain recognize words? Speed of lexical access reveals your reading brain age.
description: Measures the speed at which your brain accesses word meanings from written input. Fast lexical access is the foundation of reading fluency and verbal intelligence. This test isolates pure word recognition speed from comprehension.

seo.metaTitle: Free Word Recognition Speed Test – Reading Brain Age | ZAZAZA
seo.metaDescription: Test your word recognition speed. How fast does your brain access word meanings? Free lexical access speed test, instant results, no signup.

howToPlay: Words and non-words will flash on screen briefly.
Decide as fast as possible: real word (YES) or made-up word (NO).
Reaction time is measured for each decision.
Your average lexical decision time is your score — lower is faster.
Speed and accuracy are both measured.
content.science: Lexical decision tasks — deciding whether a letter string is a real word — are the gold standard measure of orthographic processing speed and lexical access in cognitive psychology. Response times in lexical decision tasks are highly predictive of reading fluency, verbal working memory efficiency, and crystallized intelligence. Average lexical decision RT in skilled adult readers is approximately 600-800ms. Faster access indicates more efficient mental lexicon organization and stronger orthographic representations in the left fusiform gyrus.
tips: Trust your instinct — if it feels like a word, it probably is.
Common high-frequency words (the, run, big) will feel faster than rare words.
Non-words that sound like words (e.g., 'blick') are harder — this is normal.
Reading volume predicts word recognition speed — avid readers are systematically faster.
Bilingualism can sometimes slow lexical decision due to cross-language competition.

ranks.label: S
ranks.title: Speed Reader Brain
ranks.subtitle: Processes words before they finish loading

ranks.label: A
ranks.title: Fast Lexical Access
ranks.subtitle: Reads subtitles faster than the dialogue

ranks.label: B
ranks.title: Fluent Reader
ranks.subtitle: Reads at a solid pace

ranks.label: C
ranks.title: Deliberate Reader
ranks.subtitle: Gets there. Takes their time.

ranks.label: D
ranks.title: Slow Lexical Access
ranks.subtitle: Reads menus out loud. In their head. Slowly.

title: Word Association IQ
shortDescription: What comes to mind? The speed and quality of your word associations reveals your semantic intelligence.
description: Measures the richness and speed of your semantic network — the web of word meanings and associations your brain maintains. Strong associative intelligence predicts creative thinking, verbal fluency, and problem-solving flexibility.

seo.metaTitle: Free Word Association IQ Test – Semantic Intelligence | ZAZAZA
seo.metaDescription: Test your word association speed and semantic network strength. Free verbal fluency and creative intelligence test. Instant results, no signup.

howToPlay: A word will appear on screen.
Type or select the first related word that comes to mind as fast as possible.
There are no wrong answers — speed and semantic distance are both measured.
10 rounds covering different semantic categories.
Your associative speed and semantic network breadth determine your score.
content.science: Semantic network theory (Collins & Loftus, 1975) models the mental lexicon as a network of nodes (concepts) connected by weighted edges (associations). Faster and more distant associations indicate a richer, more densely interconnected semantic network. Verbal fluency tasks — a related measure — are among the most sensitive neuropsychological tests for detecting early cognitive decline, as semantic network degradation is an early sign of neurodegenerative disease. High associative intelligence predicts creative problem-solving, metaphor comprehension, and verbal IQ.
tips: Go with your first association — deliberated responses are less diagnostic.
Don't try to be creative or clever — natural associations are more revealing.
Common associations (dog → cat) are not worse than unusual ones — both are measured.
Semantic association speed is trainable through wide reading and word games.
Bilingual individuals often show different association patterns — both are valid.
